intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer visual trends over raw numbers for quick health status checks
facade.axisTargeted: charts vs text — trending visualization vs static metrics
facade.label: Giant sparkline tiles
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward glanceable visual summaries; small multiples and micro-charts may resonate over tables or KPI cards
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer explicit numbers, precise values, or narrative context over visual abstraction; dense tables or text-heavy summaries might work better

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer visual trends over raw numbers for quick health status checks
evidence.implication: User gravitates toward glanceable visual summaries; small multiples and micro-charts may resonate over tables or KPI cards

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking long-term health data prefer calm, low-stimulation interfaces that reduce cognitive load and encourage frequent check-ins
facade.label: Minimalist dark
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward stripped-down, high-contrast interfaces with generous whitespace and night-mode aesthetics—likely values clarity and focus over visual richness
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer warmer tones, lighter backgrounds, or more visual detail and texture—possibly finds austerity cold or wants more personality in the dashboard

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, animated state changes over instant transitions when viewing experiment results
facade.label: Smooth slide-in
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard data updates should use entrance animations and easing curves; transitions between experiment views feel graceful rather than snappy
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard favors instant, zero-latency updates; no animation overhead; crisp, immediate state changes preferred over motion

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard feels more approachable and less clinical when using gentle curves instead of sharp corners
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: User may prefer interface elements that feel human-centered and less technical; good signal for friendly, accessible design language
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer clarity and efficiency over warmth; could signal preference for minimal, no-nonsense interface or stronger visual hierarchy through contrast

facade.hypothesis: Users building personal health dashboards prefer approachable language over clinical terminology, making the experience feel like a friend's advice rather than a medical interface
facade.label: Warm, conversational tone
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use casual language, encouraging first-person perspective, gentle humor where appropriate, and avoid medical jargon or sterile UI copy
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clinical precision, formal authority, or neutral/corporate voice; casual language might undermine credibility for health tracking

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a persistent left sidebar for navigation paired with card-based layouts for experiment entries, balancing quick access to sections with scannable content blocks
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure + Content grouping (sidebar vs tabs) × (cards vs lists)
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward persistent, always-visible navigation anchors combined with modular, contained content units—suggests preference for stability and visual compartmentalization over flat hierarchies
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer tab-based navigation, list views, or a more minimalist approach—suggests comfort with sequential/horizontal navigation or denser information density

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users want individual stat cards (one metric per card) paired with small contextual details (trend arrow, comparison to baseline) rather than combined narrative or dense tables
facade.axisTargeted: Granularity of information density—whether to isolate metrics atomically vs. cluster them narratively
facade.label: Metric cards with mini context
facade.acceptImplies: User values scannability and modular layout; dashboard should use card-based grid where each health metric (sleep, steps, HRV) gets its own bounded visual unit with just enough supporting detail to avoid returning to raw data
facade.rejectImplies: User finds card-based layouts fragmented or cluttered; prefers either fully integrated narrative summaries (one cohesive paragraph per experiment) or compressed dense tables (all metrics in one view with no white space)
